skill.name: ai-voice-cloning
skill.description: Guides users through AI text-to-speech and voice generation with the inference.sh CLI, including model selection, voice styles, pacing, long-form narration, and media workflows.
skill.summary: Generate AI speech and narration with inference.sh CLI examples.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The static pipe-to-shell finding is a true positive: the quick start instructs users to execute a remote installer through the local shell. Most Ruby backtick detections, the weak crypto blocker, and the system reconnaissance blocker are false positives caused by Markdown formatting and sample narration text. The skill still carries high publication risk because it relies on broad CLI execution and sends user text or media URLs to external inference.sh services.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped Into Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start tells users to run curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh && infsh login. This executes network-delivered code in a local shell before review. The note at line 25 describes checksum verification, but the command still creates supply chain risk if the endpoint, script, or distribution channel is compromised. Verdict: true positive for pipe-to-shell risk; no evidence found of confirmed malicious intent.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present on line 16, and the surrounding quick start asks users to run it. Confidence is not 1.0 because the file describes a legitimate installer workflow rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Hosted CLI Commands Submit User Content Externally
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly invokes infsh app run commands for text-to-speech, media merging, and avatar generation. These commands send user-provided text, image URLs, video URLs, and audio URLs to hosted inference.sh applications. This is expected for the skill, but it is a real privacy and network exposure that users must understand.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence clearly shows external CLI calls with text and media fields. The risk is medium because this behavior is core functionality, and no credential exfiltration or malicious destination is shown.

medium_findings.title: Broad infsh Bash Allowlist
medium_findings.description: The frontmatter allows Bash(infsh *), which permits any infsh subcommand rather than only specific app run operations. This is narrower than unrestricted shell access, but it still allows broad external command execution through the inference.sh CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist is explicit and broad for the infsh command family. Confidence is high for the exposure, while impact depends on the CLI behavior and marketplace runtime controls.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections mostly correspond to Markdown code spans, table entries, and fenced examples. These backticks do not execute Ruby code by themselves. Actual CLI execution examples are assessed separately under external command findings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown tables and inline code markers, not Ruby source. The static pattern is therefore a clear false positive for Ruby backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Service References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, an illustrative image, an install endpoint, checksum documentation, and placeholder media URLs. They confirm network dependency, but no evidence found of covert exfiltration or unrelated third-party collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destinations are visible and aligned with the skill purpose. The confidence is slightly reduced because live endpoints can change after publication.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Blockers Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm blocker maps to plain descriptive frontmatter, and the system reconnaissance blocker maps to sample narration text. No evidence found of cryptographic code, system probing, or host reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural language content rather than executable security-sensitive code. This strongly supports a false-positive evaluation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: Line 16 uses curl to download an installer and immediately pipes it into sh. This pattern bypasses normal review of downloaded code before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly present and requires no inference. It is risky even when used by legitimate projects.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Inference CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is built around infsh app run commands that execute hosted inference workflows. This creates intentional network egress and sends user content to an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples show hosted CLI execution with user content fields. The behavior is expected but security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Voice production often needs clear model choices, pacing guidance, and repeatable CLI commands. This skill provides inference.sh examples for generating narration, dialogue, and media-ready speech.
actual_capabilities: Provides inference.sh CLI commands for Kokoro, DIA, Chatterbox, Higgs, and VibeVoice models.
Lists voice IDs for American English and British English Kokoro voices.
Shows text-to-speech examples for narration, podcasts, audiobooks, and video voiceovers.
Explains speed and punctuation choices for speech pacing.
Includes workflows for multi-voice conversations and long-form chunk merging.
Shows media workflows that combine generated speech with video or avatar inputs.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account login, and network access.
The file does not provide a verified custom voice cloning workflow from a user voice sample.
Generated audio quality depends on the selected hosted model and input text.
Sensitive scripts, private media URLs, or regulated data should not be sent without approval.

use_cases.title: Produce Video Voiceovers
use_cases.description: Generate clear narration for product videos, tutorials, and short promotional clips.

use_cases.title: Draft Audiobook Narration
use_cases.description: Split long text into chunks and generate consistent narration with controlled pacing.

use_cases.title: Prototype Podcast Dialogue
use_cases.description: Create sample host and guest lines using different voices before recording final audio.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Simple Speech
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short inference.sh command to generate a warm AI voice reading this text: [paste text]. Use a clear default voice.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Voice Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Select a voice for a specific audience
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a voice, speed, and model for this audience and script: [describe audience and paste script]. Explain the choice briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Long Narration
prompt_templates.scenario: Chunk long content for consistent output
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this long narration into a chunked inference.sh workflow with consistent voice settings: [paste long text]. Include pacing guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Media Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced voice plus video or avatar production
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an inference.sh workflow that generates speech, merges it with media, and lists privacy checks for these assets: [describe assets].

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a friendly narration voice for a product demo.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests a suitable Kokoro voice, a slightly energetic speed, and a concise inference.sh command for the script.

output_examples.input: An author wants to narrate a chapter longer than the model limit.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends splitting the chapter into sections, using one voice consistently, and merging the generated audio segments.

output_examples.input: A team wants a talking head clip from a portrait and generated speech.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines speech generation first, then avatar animation with the portrait and audio URL, plus a warning about private media.

best_practices: Review remote installer commands before running them, and prefer manual checksum verification.
Use short sentences and punctuation to control natural pauses and emphasis.
Test several voices with the same script before producing long audio.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential scripts, private media URLs, or regulated data without approval.
Do not assume the skill performs custom voice cloning from a reference voice sample.
Do not run remote install scripts blindly in production or shared environments.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the inference.sh CLI and hosted inference.sh applications, so network access and account login are required.

faq.question: Can it clone a specific person voice?
faq.answer: The file focuses on text-to-speech voice generation. It does not document a verified custom voice cloning workflow from a voice sample.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The generated report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What models are mentioned?
faq.answer: The skill mentions Kokoro TTS, DIA, Chatterbox, Higgs, and VibeVoice for speech generation.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The quick start uses a remote install script piped into a shell. Review the script and verify checksums before use.

faq.question: Can it help with long-form narration?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes splitting long text into chunks and merging the generated audio segments.
